attributes.stem: A fit and well 65-year-old man attends his General Practice for a general health check. During the interview, he asks if he should have any screening tests, such as one for prostate cancer. He does not have any urinary symptoms, but states his father was diagnosed with prostate cancer at the age of 70.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?
options: Recommend a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) test and a digital rectal examination
Refer to a urologist for further assessment
Reassure him that no screening is required
Discuss the pros and cons of a prostate-specific antigen (PSA) test
attributes.explanation:
Current Australian guidelines recommend a discussion of the pros and cons of PSA screening between the patient and doctor for asymptomatic men aged between 50-70 years. Screening for prostate cancer is a controversial topic due to ongoing disagreement between the potential benefits and harms. These pros and cons are essential for discussion between the patient and doctor to determine whether to undergo screening.

  • Pros: Localised early prostate cancer rarely causes symptoms, making it challenging to diagnose at an early stage, which is one of the main arguments for screening. Prostate cancer screening reduces the relative risk of dying from prostate cancer by 30% (25-31% ERSPC trial, 35% Goteborg trial with sub arm RR 50% for 50-59 yo). Hence, there is a growing amount of evidence favouring prostate cancer screening.

  • Cons: It is important to acknowledge that PSA has a very low specificity (27%) and can be elevated in a number of common benign conditions, such as benign prostate hyperplasia. Therefore, testing in the absence of clinical suspicion may lead to unnecessary distress and investigation.

    • PSA specificity for prostate cancer can be increased by adjuncts such as PSA density and Free to Total PSA ratio measures.

Stating that the above patient requires no form of screening without appropriate discussion is inappropriate given that there is level one evidence that demonstrates PSA testing in men aged 50-69 reduces prostate cancer-specific mortality and the incidence of metastatic prostate cancer. In making an individual risk assessment, the following factors should be considered:

  • age of the patient

  • family history or prostate or breast cancer

  • African or African-American descent

This particular patient is aged 65 and has a family history of prostate cancer. A PSA examination should be recommended and if this is elevated, multiparametric magnetic resonance imaging considered for biopsy optimisation.

A digital rectal examination is not recommended as a stand-alone screening test for prostate cancer.

Referral to a urologist is not necessary at this stage without initial screening discussions and potential PSA testing. A referral would be more appropriate if there were abnormal findings or elevated PSA levels.
learningPoints: PSA is not specific to prostate cancer and can be elevated in benign conditions.
PSA screening should be offered for men between the ages of 50 and 70 after a careful discussion regarding the pros and cons of screening.
references: Schröder FH, Hugosson J, Roobol MJ, Tammela TL, Zappa M, Nelen V, Kwiatkowski M, Lujan M, Määttänen L, Lilja H, Denis LJ, Recker F, Paez A, Bangma CH, Carlsson S, Puliti D, Villers A, Rebillard X, Hakama M, Stenman UH, Kujala P, Taari K, Aus G, Huber A, van der Kwast TH, van Schaik RH, de Koning HJ, Moss SM, Auvinen A; ERSPC Investigators. Screening and prostate cancer mortality: results of the European Randomised Study of Screening for Prostate Cancer (ERSPC) at 13 years of follow-up. Lancet. 2014 Dec 6;384(9959):2027-35. doi: 10.1016/S0140-6736(14)60525-0. Epub 2014 Aug 6. PMID: 25108889; PMCID: PMC4427906. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/25108889/

Hugosson J, Carlsson S, Aus G, Bergdahl S, Khatami A, Lodding P, Pihl CG, Stranne J, Holmberg E, Lilja H. Mortality results from the Göteborg randomised population-based prostate-cancer screening trial. Lancet Oncol. 2010 Aug;11(8):725-32. doi: 10.1016/S1470-2045(10)70146-7. Epub 2010 Jul 2. PMID: 20598634; PMCID: PMC4089887. Available from: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/20598634/

Jain MA, Leslie SW, Sapra A. Prostate Cancer Screening. [Updated 2023 Oct 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2025 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK556081/

Cornford P, van den Bergh RCN, Briers E, et al. EAU-EANM-ESTRO-ESUR-ISUP-SIOG Guidelines on Prostate Cancer—2024 Update. Part I: Screening, Diagnosis, and Local Treatment with Curative Intent. Eur Urol. 2024; 86:148-63.

DRAFT 2025 Guidelines for the Early Detection of Prostate Cancer in Australia. https://www.pcfa.org.au/media/f1ubqz10/draft-2025-guidelines-for-the-early-detection-of-prostate-cancer-in-australia_public-consultation_april-14-2025.pdf
